text:
"" Ash-Shaikh al-Allmah, Slih al-Fawzn (may Allh preserve him): 

Advice to students of knowledge who busy themselves with Takfr, Tabd and seeking out the slips of the scholars and the Salafi callers to Allh, claiming that this is the path of Shaikh al-Fawzn and others from the people of knowledge. 

A summary of the Shaikh al-Fawzns speech: 

This is a trial, and not permissible: busying oneself with the mistakes of the people, and causing people to flee from the scholars, the students of knowledge, and from the callers to Allh. This is a fitnahit is not permissible to act upon that, nor to follow the one who does that. 

Rather, it is obligatory for there to be mutual advisingthe Religion is sincere adviceand cooperation upon righteousness and piety between the Muslims, and to act with knowledge. 

As for busying oneself with this person and that person, that is backbiting and repellingand backbiting is among the major sinsand it does not rectify anything; instead, it corrupts and splits. 

If we see something in a person, we advise him, either by writing to him or by speaking to him. As for sitting and backbiting, that is harm. This leads to the splitting of the Muslims and causes people to flee from seeking knowledge. This behaviour is not permissible at all. 

They are lying, those who say that this is our way, or the way of others [from the scholars]indeed, we warn against this in our lectures, our books, and we warn against this path.

Recorded about 12 years ago. And remind, for verily, the reminding benefits the believers. (Adh-Dhariyat: 55)"
"

Abu khadeejah literally makes tabdee on the vast majority of salafi scholarship.

text:
"Ali ibn al-Ja'd narrated to us: Mahbb az-Zhid nformed: al-Hasan [al-Basri] said:

Indeed, I encountered people (Sahba) for whom a garment was never folded (i.e. stored away carefully or possessed in excess), nor did any one of them ever crave a desire upon his family (for worldly pleasures), nor did any of them ever order (his family) to prepare food (for himself) , nor did any one of them ever divide an inheritance with his brother. Indeed, one of them would have an inheritance share between him and his brother and he would say: 'It is yours.' He did not love to occupy himself with anything from worldly affairs. Indeed, one of them would eat a meal and wish that it would remain in his stomach (undigested) just as a brick remains in water, so that it would be his provision from the world.

{Kitb al-J [109]}"

text:
"Imam al-Dhahabi: May Allah Curse Those Who Spread Slander and Namimah

Certain individuals conveyed false reports to Imam Ibn Khuzaymah concerning some of his companions and students, leading him to declare them liars and to warn against them on the basis of what had reached him.

After mentioning this story and what Ibn Khuzaymah said about them, Imam al-Dhahabi commented: 

They are not liars; rather, they are reliable Imams. The Shaykh only spoke according to what was conveyed to him about them. So may Allah curse those who spread slander, and those who go about with namimah (tale-carrying).

(Siyar Alam al-Nubala 14/380)"

text:
""A Concise Summary on the Concurrence of Jumuah and Eid 

Sheikh Saleem Al-Hilli afidhahullh states:

All praise is due to Allh, Lord of the Worlds, and peace and blessings be upon the Master of the Messengers, Muammad, and upon his family, companions, and those who follow them with excellence until the Day of Judgment.

My dear brothers and sisters...There has been much discussion regarding this matter (i.e. Concurrence of Jumuah and Eid). Some have asked: Does one prayer suffice for the other, or is it obligatory for a Muslim to perform both the Eid and Jumuah prayers?

Many who have spoken on this issue have done so without the requisite qualification to understand the evidences transmitted from the Messenger of Allh . This is because they approach the matter based on a flawed methodology: they first adopt a belief, then seek out supporting evidence. This is a mistake! The correct methodology is to derive beliefs from evidence, not to impose ones beliefs or schools of thought upon the evidence.

Therefore, I shall briefly present the adths and reports attributed to the Messenger of Allh  and the noble Companionsmay Allh be pleased with themthen explain the positions of the recognised schools of Fiqh on this issue, relying upon Allh Almighty.

So I say, with Allhs guidance, that there are both prophetic traditions (marf) and reports from the companion (mawqf) on this matter.

The first is the adth of Zayd ibn Arqam (may Allh be pleased with him), in which Muwiyah ibn Ab Sufyn (may Allh be pleased with him) asked him: Did you witness the Messenger of Allh  having two Eids occur on the same day? He replied, Yes. He asked, What did he do? Zayd said, He performed the Eid prayer, then granted a concession regarding the Jumuah prayer, saying: Whoever wishes to pray, let him pray. This adth was narrated by Amad, Ab Dwd, An-Nas, Ibn Mjah, Ad-Drim, and Al-Hkim, and it is authentic.

The second adth is from Ab Hurayrah (may Allh be pleased with him), in which the Messenger of Allh  said: Two Eids have coincided on this day of yours. Whoever wishes, it will suffice him in place of Jumuah. But we will indeed perform the Jumuah. This adth was also narrated by Ab Dwd and Ibn Mjah, and it is authentic.

The third adth is from Ibn Umar (may Allh be pleased with him), who said: Two Eids coincided during the time of the Messenger of Allh . He led the people in prayer, then said: Whoever wishes to attend the Jumuah may do so, and whoever wishes to refrain may do so. This adth was narrated by Ibn Mjah. A-abarn also transmitted it with the wording: Two Eids coincided during the time of the Messenger of Allh  on the day of Eid Al-Fir and Friday. The Messenger of Allh  led them in the Eid prayer, then turned to face them and said: O people, you have achieved good and reward. Indeed, we are holding the Jumuah. Whoever wishes to join us may do so, and whoever wishes to return to his family may do so. This narration is also authentic.

The fourth adth is from Ibn Abbs (may Allh be pleased with them both), in which the Messenger of Allh  said: Two Eids have coincided on this day of yours. Whoever wishes, it will suffice him in place of Jumuah. But we are gathering, in-sh-Allh. This adth is also authentic and narrated by Ibn Mjah.

From the reports of the Companions: A ibn Ab Rab said: Ibn Az-Zubayr led us in the Eid prayer on a Friday early in the day. Then we proceeded to attend Jumuah, but he did not come out to us, so we prayed individually. Ibn Abbs was in A-if, and when he returned and we mentioned this to him, he said: He acted in accordance with the Sunnah. This was reported by Ab Dwd, and by Ibn Khuzaymah with a different wording and with an addition: Ibn Az-Zubayr said: I saw Umar ibn Al-Khab, when two Eids coincided, do the same."
"
--------------

I would like to mention the post below by Shaykh Salim al-Hilali   is amazing as he mentions many evidences and keeps it concise. But people are lazy they would rather make jokes on social media about Jumuah and Eid being the same day rather than reading.

Here is also Sh Sulayman ar-Ruhayli   speaking about it

text:
"Allmah Ibn Taymiyyah was asked about saying L Ilha ill Allh 70,000 times and gifting it to the dead. He responded: If he does it like this 70,000 times, or more, or less, and gifts it Allh will benefit them with it. And this has no [basis] in neither authentic nor weak Hadth.

 [Majm al-Fatw, 24/323, translation from @ahnaafservices Twitter]

Benefits:

1. Ihda As-Sawab (Urdu Isaal e Sawab), or gifting deeds to the dead, is something that was generally considered permissible.

2. It's not necessary that every particular deed related to Sadaqah Jariyah is proven from the Sahabah. For ex. It's not necessary to show that the Sahabah donated an X-ray machine to a hospital with the intention of Sadaqah Jariyah.

3. A precedent in the actions of the Sahabah. - Summary- Unable to financially contribute to the Tabuk expedition, Ulbah bin Zayd (Radi Allahu Anhu) wept in prayer and sincerely offered his personal honor as charity by forgiving any Muslim who had wronged him. The next morning, the Prophet (Salallahu Alaihi Wa Sallam) informed him that Allah had recognized his pure intention and officially accepted his act of forgiveness as a highly rewarded charity. (Seerat Ibn Hisham (Vol. 2, p. 516) and Zaad al-Ma'ad by Ibn al-Qayyim (Vol. 3, p. 461), authentic.)

4. This also means, reciting the Quran and gifting it to the dead is permissible. Ibn Taymiyyah held that opinion. In our contemporary Ulama, Shaykh Muhammad bin Saleh Al-Uthaymeen said that it was permissible to gift deeds to the dead."

text:
"This is my will and my final message. If these words reach you, know that Israel has succeeded in killing me and silencing my voice. First, peace be upon you and Allahs mercy and blessings.

Allah knows I gave every effort and all my strength to be a support and a voice for my people, ever since I opened my eyes to life in the alleys and streets of the Jabalia refugee camp. My hope was that Allah would extend my life so I could return with my family and loved ones to our original town of occupied Asqalan (Al-Majdal). But Allahs will came first, and His decree is final. I have lived through pain in all its details, tasted suffering and loss many times, yet I never once hesitated to convey the truth as it is, without distortion or falsificationso that Allah may bear witness against those who stayed silent, those who accepted our killing, those who choked our breath, and whose hearts were unmoved by the scattered remains of our children and women, doing nothing to stop the massacre that our people have faced for more than a year and a half.

I entrust you with Palestinethe jewel in the crown of the Muslim world, the heartbeat of every free person in this world. I entrust you with its people, with its wronged and innocent children who never had the time to dream or live in safety and peace. Their pure bodies were crushed under thousands of tons of Israeli bombs and missiles, torn apart and scattered across the walls.

I urge you not to let chains silence you, nor borders restrain you. Be bridges toward the liberation of the land and its people, until the sun of dignity and freedom rises over our stolen homeland. I entrust you to take care of my family. I entrust you with my beloved daughter Sham, the light of my eyes, whom I never got the chance to watch grow up as I had dreamed.

I entrust you with my dear son Salah, whom I had wished to support and accompany through life until he grew strong enough to carry my burden and continue the mission.

I entrust you with my beloved mother, whose blessed prayers brought me to where I am, whose supplications were my fortress and whose light guided my path. I pray that Allah grants her strength and rewards her on my behalf with the best of rewards.

I also entrust you with my lifelong companion, my beloved wife, Umm Salah (Bayan), from whom the war separated me for many long days and months. Yet she remained faithful to our bond, steadfast as the trunk of an olive tree that does not bendpatient, trusting in Allah, and carrying the responsibility in my absence with all her strength and faith.

I urge you to stand by them, to be their support after Allah Almighty. If I die, I die steadfast upon my principles. I testify before Allah that I am content with His decree, certain of meeting Him, and assured that what is with Allah is better and everlasting.

O Allah, accept me among the martyrs, forgive my past and future sins, and make my blood a light that illuminates the path of freedom for my people and my family. Forgive me if I have fallen short, and pray for me with mercy, for I kept my promise and never changed or betrayed it.

Do not forget Gaza And do not forget me in your sincere prayers for forgiveness and acceptance.

Anas Jamal Al-Sharif
06.04.2025

This is what our beloved Anas requested to be published upon his martyrdom.",

text:
"Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar    was among the most devoted, scrupulous, and steadfast Companions of the Messenger of Allh . He was a man whose life became a living embodiment of the Sunnah, so precise in his adherence that he would follow even the smallest actions of the Prophet  out of love and reverence.

He was Abdullh ibn Umar ibn al-Khab al-Adaw al-Qurash, the son of the great Caliph Sayyidun Umar ibn al-Khab   . He was born in Makkah before the Hijrah and embraced Islm at a young age alongside his noble father. He emigrated to al-Madnah al-Munawwarah at the age of ten, and thus was counted among the early Muhjirn, those whom Allh, Most High, praised in His Book:

And the foremost, the first of the Muhjirn and the Anr and those who followed them with excellence, Allh is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great success.

[Qurn 9:100]

In his youth, he longed to participate in battle alongside the Messenger of Allh . He was presented at Badr but was turned back due to his young age. He was again presented at Uud, when he was only thirteen years old, hoping to join the ranks, but he was sent back by the Prophet  due to his youth, alongside Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd   . That moment weighed heavily upon him, and he later said: 

A night like that never came my way, filled with sleeplessness, sadness, and weeping, because the Messenger of Allh  did not accept me.

After returning him from Uud, the Messenger of Allh  appointed him among those tasked with guarding the children in Madnah. 

Yet his heart remained attached to striving in the path of Allh, and his sincere supplication to be permitted to fight was soon answered. In the fifth year after Hijrah, when he reached the age of fifteen, he was allowed to participate in the Battle of al-Khandaq. 

From that point forward, he took part in the major campaigns of Islm. He participated in the campaign against Ban Qurayah, the journey to al-udaybiyyah, and was among those who pledged beneath the tree in the Pledge of al-Riwn, a moment honored by Allh, Most High:

Indeed, Allh was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with a near victory.

[Qurn 48:18]

He later joined the campaigns of Khaybar, Mutah, the Conquest of Makkah, the expedition against Ban Judhaymah, the Battle of unayn, and the march to Tabk. He was also among the soldiers of the army of Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd   . 

After returning from this expedition, he volunteered in the wars against apostasy and had the honor of participating in the Battle of Yammah against Musaylamah al-Kadhdhb. 

He later marched to Shm and fought at al-Yarmk, then joined the campaigns in the Persian lands under Sayyidun Sad ibn Ab Waqq   , attending al-Qdisiyyah, Jall, and the conquest of al-Madin. He also took part in the conquest of Egypt and campaigns in North Africa. 

Even in his later years, he joined the expedition toward Constantinople, about which the Prophet  said: The first army to attack the city of Caesar is forgiven.

What distinguished Ibn Umar    above all was his extraordinary commitment to following the Sunnah. He would carefully observe where the Prophet  prayed, where he sat, and even the paths he walked, then imitate them with remarkable precision. 

If he saw the Prophet  perform prayer in a particular place, he would later pray in that very same place. If he saw him make supplication while standing, he would supplicate while standing; and if he saw him invoke Allh while sitting, he would do the same. His life was not merely one of narration, but of complete imitation.

He was among the foremost scholars of the Companions and a prolific narrator of hadith, transmitting over 2,600 narrations. His knowledge was paired with deep caution, he was known for his reluctance to issue legal verdicts without certainty, and he preferred silence over speculation. 

Due to his age, knowledge, and unwavering adherence to the Sunnah, he became known in his time as the Shaykh of the abah, and even powerful governors such as al-ajjj ibn Ysuf treated him with respect and deference due to his immense status amongst the Muslims. From the greatest testimonies to his precision in transmitting the Sunnah is that he stands at the head of one of the most authentic chains in hadith, the Golden Chain.

Mlik  Nfi  Ibn Umar.

A generation of leading Tbin took knowledge from him, including Sad ibn al-Musayyib, Thbit al-Bunn, ws, al-Zuhr, Muammad ibn Srn, Nfi (his freed servant), Mujhid, and many others. His influence thus extended deeply into the next generation of scholarship. 

His legacy was also carried forward through his own household, as several of his sons became scholars, most notably Slim ibn Abdullh ibn Umar, who was counted among the great jurists of Madnah. Through both his students and his family, his knowledge and methodology continued to shape the transmission of the Sunnah for generations.

During the periods of civil strife, having lived to witness both the First and Second Fitnah among the Muslims, Ibn Umar    consistently adopted a principled stance of restraint. He presented himself at the sittings of arbitration, demonstrating his desire for unity among the Muslims. 

He famously said: When you call me to prayer, I respond. However, when you call me to fight a Muslim, I do not. He never raised his sword against the believers. Yet, on the other hand, he answered every call to strive against the disbelievers, embodying the words of Allh, Most High:

Humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allh 

[Qurn 5:54]

He was known for his humility, generosity, and detachment from worldly life. Wealth would come to him, yet he would spend it freely in charity. He freed many slaves and cared deeply for the poor and needy. His nights were often spent in prayer, and his days in fasting and remembrance.

Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar    passed away in Makkah in 73 AH and was buried near Dh uw, leaving behind a legacy of unwavering adherence to the Sunnah and a life defined by sincerity and caution.

May Allh be pleased with Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar   , the devoted follower of the Sunnah and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions of the Messenger of Allh ."

He emigrated to al-Madnah al-Munawwarah at the age of ten, and thus was counted among the early Muhjirn, those whom Allh, Most High, praised in His Book: And the foremost, the first of the Muhjirn and the Anr and those who followed them with excellence, Allh is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great success. [Qurn 9:100] In his youth, he longed to participate in battle alongside the Messenger of Allh . He was presented at Badr but was turned back due to his young age. He was again presented at Uud, when he was only thirteen years old, hoping to join the ranks, but he was sent back by the Prophet due to his youth, alongside Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd . That moment weighed heavily upon him, and he later said: A night like that never came my way, filled with sleeplessness, sadness, and weeping, because the Messenger of Allh did not accept me. After returning him from Uud, the Messenger of Allh appointed him among those tasked with guarding the children in Madnah. Yet his heart remained attached to striving in the path of Allh, and his sincere supplication to be permitted to fight was soon answered. In the fifth year after Hijrah, when he reached the age of fifteen, he was allowed to participate in the Battle of al-Khandaq. From that point forward, he took part in the major campaigns of Islm. He participated in the campaign against Ban Qurayah, the journey to al-udaybiyyah, and was among those who pledged beneath the tree in the Pledge of al-Riwn, a moment honored by Allh, Most High: Indeed, Allh was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with a near victory. [Qurn 48:18] He later joined the campaigns of Khaybar, Mutah, the Conquest of Makkah, the expedition against Ban Judhaymah, the Battle of unayn, and the march to Tabk. He was also among the soldiers of the army of Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd . After returning from this expedition, he volunteered in the wars against apostasy and had the honor of participating in the Battle of Yammah against Musaylamah al-Kadhdhb. He later marched to Shm and fought at al-Yarmk, then joined the campaigns in the Persian lands under Sayyidun Sad ibn Ab Waqq , attending al-Qdisiyyah, Jall, and the conquest of al-Madin. He also took part in the conquest of Egypt and campaigns in North Africa. Even in his later years, he joined the expedition toward Constantinople, about which the Prophet said: The first army to attack the city of Caesar is forgiven. What distinguished Ibn Umar above all was his extraordinary commitment to following the Sunnah. He would carefully observe where the Prophet prayed, where he sat, and even the paths he walked, then imitate them with remarkable precision. If he saw the Prophet perform prayer in a particular place, he would later pray in that very same place. If he saw him make supplication while standing, he would supplicate while standing; and if he saw him invoke Allh while sitting, he would do the same. His life was not merely one of narration, but of complete imitation. He was among the foremost scholars of the Companions and a prolific narrator of hadith, transmitting over 2,600 narrations. His knowledge was paired with deep caution, he was known for his reluctance to issue legal verdicts without certainty, and he preferred silence over speculation. Due to his age, knowledge, and unwavering adherence to the Sunnah, he became known in his time as the Shaykh of the abah, and even powerful governors such as al-ajjj ibn Ysuf treated him with respect and deference due to his immense status amongst the Muslims. From the greatest testimonies to his precision in transmitting the Sunnah is that he stands at the head of one of the most authentic chains in hadith, the Golden Chain. Mlik Nfi Ibn Umar. A generation of leading Tbin took knowledge from him, including Sad ibn al-Musayyib, Thbit al-Bunn, ws, al-Zuhr, Muammad ibn Srn, Nfi (his freed servant), Mujhid, and many others. His influence thus extended deeply into the next generation of scholarship. His legacy was also carried forward through his own household, as several of his sons became scholars, most notably Slim ibn Abdullh ibn Umar, who was counted among the great jurists of Madnah. Through both his students and his family, his knowledge and methodology continued to shape the transmission of the Sunnah for generations. During the periods of civil strife, having lived to witness both the First and Second Fitnah among the Muslims, Ibn Umar consistently adopted a principled stance of restraint. He presented himself at the sittings of arbitration, demonstrating his desire for unity among the Muslims. He famously said: When you call me to prayer, I respond. However, when you call me to fight a Muslim, I do not. He never raised his sword against the believers. Yet, on the other hand, he answered every call to strive against the disbelievers, embodying the words of Allh, Most High: Humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allh [Qurn 5:54] He was known for his humility, generosity, and detachment from worldly life. Wealth would come to him, yet he would spend it freely in charity. He freed many slaves and cared deeply for the poor and needy. His nights were often spent in prayer, and his days in fasting and remembrance. Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar passed away in Makkah in 73 AH and was buried near Dh uw, leaving behind a legacy of unwavering adherence to the Sunnah and a life defined by sincerity and caution. May Allh be pleased with Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar , the devoted follower of the Sunnah and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions of the Messenger of Allh ."

text:
"it is not permissible for a person to become a legal consultant in man-made laws, or to study in such laws in institutions that teach them, or to enter colleges that teach man-made laws and disbelief in Allah, may Allah protect us and you from that, as exists in some countries.
 
You find an ignorant person with no knowledge studying law. First, he should learn the Shariah of Allah and know it well. Whoever learns the Shariah and becomes knowledgeable may refute the falsehood of the Kuffar. But for a person to say he is studying what the Kuffar brought and similar matters, this is misguidance, may Allah protect us and you.

There is nothing in Islam called a college of law in this sense; rather, there are Shariah colleges that teach the Book and the Sunnah. These are the legitimate institutions that teach the law of Allah.

Shaykh Abdullah as Sa'd (may Allah free him) -Explanation of Sunan al-Tirmidhi, Kitab al-Salah,Lesson 73",

text:
"'    

The Ashari view of Gods actions is rooted in a strict form of divine command theory (DCT), where no logically possible act is deemed unbefitting of Him. This approach raises deeper questions about the nature of truth and divine guidance. 

For example, on the Ash'ari view, Does God have to tell the truth? This question gets to the core of a deep problem that underlies their strict DCT view. 

Ill break it down in simple terms here. But first, a disclaimer:

                .               .

 B:

- Asharis believe that no logically possible act is unbefitting of God. Any action you can possibly conceive of is an equally possible and valid expression of His will as any other. On this view, there is nothing about God that makes the creation of this world any more likely than a world with nothing but a pile of dog feces created for absolutely no reason (excuse the stark example, but it helps illustrate the point clearly). He chose to create this world instead of the feces world for literally no reason at all.

- For example, on this view, a world that contains nothing but an innocent baby suffering in Hell for an eternity for absolutely no reason is perfectly befitting of God and His perfection. It is axiologically identical to a world where the righteous are rewarded and only the sinful are punished. Both possible realities are equally befitting of God, and theres absolutely no reason or wisdom that can explain why He chose to do one instead of the other.

- Even in terms of our deeds: God doesnt prefer one deed over another, unless by prefer you mean He just arbitrarily willed for it to happen (for no reason). The most pious worshipper to have ever lived is identical to Iblis in terms of Gods preference or love. Unless we understand love as Gods will to reward the worshipper, which again, is completely arbitrary and could have gone in the exact opposite direction with the worshiper getting punished and Iblis getting rewarded. Both equally possible and equally befitting of God. The only reason we assume that one is better than the other is because thats what weve become accustomed to.

So, on the Ashari view, Gods actions are completely arbitrary. He doesnt act for any specific reason. He creates clouds through which He creates rain, but He didnt create the clouds for that reason. It just happens to be the case that he created clouds followed by rain.

  

So, where does the truthfulness question come in?

Well, if all logically possible acts are equally befitting of God, and theres nothing about Him that explains why He chooses to act one way instead of another except for brute arbitrary will, then how can you be sure He tells the truth?

They cant appeal to perfection, as Al-Juwayni and others explain, because then the entire foundation collapses and we could (as we do) similarly appeal to perfection when explaining why any of the above possibilities is unbefitting of God.

The typical Ashari move here is to argue that its logically impossible for God to lie. They say that Gods created speech (like the Arabic Qur'an) necessarily indicates His internal speech.

Therefore, lying is impossible because created speech cannot conflict with the internal meaning it indicates.

But even if thats true, it doesnt solve the real issue, namely: How do you know  that this SPECIFIC instance of "created" speechlike the Qur'ancorresponds to Gods internal speech?

  " "

In response, Asharis would typically appeal to miracles. 

The idea is that when you see a prophet perform miracles, God creates an unshakable certainty in your mind that the prophet is truthful.

The logic is that miracles are like a seal of approval from God proving that the prophet speaks the truth.

They use the common analogy of the king to explain this:

Imagine a king in a grand gathering. A man stands up and says, Im the kings messenger and will speak on behalf of him. To prove it, the man turns to the king and says, O King, if Im truthful, stand up and sit back down as a sign. When the king, who doesnt usually do such a thing, actually complies, everyone in the room instantly knows the man is truthful.

Asharis argue that this analogous to what happens when God displays miracles through a prophet. It creates undeniable certainty that the prophet is truthful.

And of course, this is true in general, but not on Ashari grounds!

Why? Because they also believe that God acts for no reasons whatsoever and that any logically conceivable act is in line with His perfection.

This analogy only works if the king acts with purpose; if he has "reasons" behind his actions. If the kings actions are just randomif he stands up and sits down for no reason whatsoeverthen the act doesnt prove anything.

He couldn't have done it with the intention of demonstrating the mans truthfulness because he never does one thing with the intention of achieving another in the first place.

So, God has the ability to create miracles at the hands if a false prophet, and on the Ashari view, whether He would choose to do so or not is entirely arbitrary: no one act is more preferable or likely than the other with respect to His nature and perfection.

And as Ibn Taymiyya says, appealing to "necessity" to justify knowing one thing over another, when theres no objective difference between the two whatsoever, ultimately destroys any reliable basis for knowledge.

 " " 

Finally, one of the most common responses they present is that if false prophets were allowed to perform miracles, it would undermine Gods ability to reveal truth to humanity.

They reason that if false prophets could perform miracles, it would blur the line between truth and falsehood, making it impossible for God to guide people to the truth through miracles.

So they argue that this would compromise Gods omnipotence, as it would entail that He is unable to demonstrate the truthfulness of a prophet, and therefore, it could never happen.

However, this argument falls apart when examined against their broader theological framework. Heres why:

1.       

The first issue is that the Ashari view itself makes the very act of demonstrating a prophets truthfulness through miracles impossible. Their framework denies that God acts with purpose or intention, which means there is no reason behind any action He performs. But for miracles to serve as evidence of prophethood, there must be a purposeful link between the miracle and the claim it validates.

For example, if a king sends a messenger with a unique seal to prove his authority, the seal serves as evidence only because the king intentionally assigned it that role. Without intention, the seal is meaningless.

Similarly, miracles cannot inherently signify truthfulness unless God intentionally creates them for that purpose.

Since the Ashari view denies that God acts with reasons, it follows that demonstrating truthfulness through miracles is inherently impossible in their framework. And if an act is inherently impossible, divine power does not "attach" to itjust as it does not "attach" to creating a square circle.

This makes their argument self-destruct. If their framework already excludes the possibility of demonstrating truth through miracles, then allowing miracles for false prophets would not compromise Gods power, as it cannot attach to something impossible in the first place.

2.    () 

When confronted with the problem of intentionality, some Asharis respond by appealing to talazumthe idea that one thing can logically necessitate another. 

They argue that miracles could inherently serve as evidence of truthfulness through a built-in necessary connection, without requiring any purposeful act from God. For example, they might say that just as knowledge necessitates life, a miracle could necessitate the truthfulness of a prophets claim.

But this argument fails because miracles do not intrinsically signify truthfulness. Their role as evidence of genuine prophethood relies on being intentionally assigned that role by God. Without such purpose or reason, a miracle could just as easily be a random act of power, with no connection to the truth of a prophets claim.

Think of it like a flag on a battlefield. A flag might signify victory, but only if it is intentionally raised for that purpose. If the flag is raised randomly or blown into the air by the wind, it no longer serves as a meaningful indicator of victory.

Similarly, for miracles to signify the truthfulness of a prophet, there must be a purposeful act linking the miracle to its role as evidence.

Without any such reasons behind God's actions, there is no necessary connection between miracles and truthfulness. Their appeal to talazum collapses because the connection between a miracle and its meaning is not intrinsic; it must be purposefully assigned.

3.       

This "compromised power" response also leads to a deeper dilemma: is it possible for God to allow a false prophet to perform miracles, or not?

They are left with two options, both of which are problematic:

)            :

This position is correct, because it aligns with the idea that Gods wisdom and perfection would never permit the validation of falsehood. However, it also contradicts their framework, which denies that any act can be inherently unbefitting or inappropriate for God.

For them, all logically possible acts are equally befitting of God's perfection, so they cannot consistently claim that God would never allow miracles at the hands of a false prophet without such an act being impossible in itself.

)           :

This position compromises Gods omnipotence by making something possible in itself (creating miracles at the hands of a false prophet) impossible for God. It implies a deficiency in divine power.
----------------------------------

In short, the Ashari stance on the arbitrariness of Gods actions, along with their belief that no possible act is "unbefitting" of Him, leads to serious foundational issues with the concepts of truth and divine guidance."

B: - Asharis believe that no logically possible act is unbefitting of God. Any action you can possibly conceive of is an equally possible and valid expression of His will as any other. On this view, there is nothing about God that makes the creation of this world any more likely than a world with nothing but a pile of dog feces created for absolutely no reason (excuse the stark example, but it helps illustrate the point clearly). Both possible realities are equally befitting of God, and theres absolutely no reason or wisdom that can explain why He chose to do one instead of the other. - Even in terms of our deeds: God doesnt prefer one deed over another, unless by prefer you mean He just arbitrarily willed for it to happen (for no reason). The most pious worshipper to have ever lived is identical to Iblis in terms of Gods preference or love. Unless we understand love as Gods will to reward the worshipper, which again, is completely arbitrary and could have gone in the exact opposite direction with the worshiper getting punished and Iblis getting rewarded. Both equally possible and equally befitting of God. The only reason we assume that one is better than the other is because thats what weve become accustomed to. So, on the Ashari view, Gods actions are completely arbitrary. He doesnt act for any specific reason. He creates clouds through which He creates rain, but He didnt create the clouds for that reason. It just happens to be the case that he created clouds followed by rain. So, where does the truthfulness question come in? text:
Ar-Rabah ibn Ab Abdir-Ramn said: 

Its not befitting for anyone who knows that he has something from knowledge to ruin himself. 

[Al-Madkhal (no. 687) of al-Bayhaq: a] 

Shaykh Muammad al-Uthaymn said: 

This is a good statement. How [does one ruin himself]? By neglecting the knowledge Allh gave him. Wasting knowledge occurs through neglecting it - not maintaining or caring about it. It also occurs by abandoning acting upon it i.e. that a person maintains it [through revision] but doesnt act upon it; this person is considered to have wasted knowledge, meaning that they didnt benefit from it. 

This should be an admonition for every student of knowledge to maintain what Allah granted them of knowledge. How? By reviewing, revising and acting upon it. For this reason, some [of the scholars] said, Preserve knowledge by acting upon it, while others said, Preserve it by writing it down, and both are correct [as one should do both]. 

(Shar a al-Bukhr)

text:
"The Prophet  said:      (There is no marriage without the permission of the wal). Sunan Ab Dwd 2805, h (al-Albn).

Ikhwanis: Parent/guardian can be present in a marriage sit-down, but they are requested not to interfere with the sessions.

So the guardian cannot "interfere", and as to add to the humiliation, he should pay a fee to "observe", whilst his daughter is in a "rotational setting" meeting other men. He is the wal, he should be jealous over his ward and not exhibit beta-male energy.

For the people who lazily proclaim: Why do you Salafis busy yourself with refutations?

This is why. We observe nonsense like this daily and out of love for Kitb and Sunnah and love for the truth to be upheld, we speak out. This is the vigilance every Muslim should have in defending the Sunnah and uniting the people upon it.

Porting speed dating concepts into Islamic marriage meet-ups is humiliation and ignorance, adopting the methods of the disbelievers. It reminds us of the narration:

Ab Hurayrah (Allah be pleased with him) narrated that the Prophet () said: The Hour will not be established until my Ummah will take the ways of those generations (nations) that preceded them, following them handspan by handspan, cubit-length by cubit-length. They asked: O Allahs Messengers! Such as the Persians and Romans? He replied: Who else except for them. (al-Bukhr, no. 7319)

We ask Allh for safety.",

text:
"Every Muslim who is being slandered by the hypocrites/disbelievers should be defended without a doubt, or offered a word of support at a minimum (even if they dont need it). This is the bare minimum rights that any of the Mashayikh, Mujahideen, or the laymen Muslims have upon their Muslim brothers.

That being said, do not be of those who voluntarily bring heartache or sadness to Muslims, by sharing with them slander against them, that they might otherwise be ignorant of occurring. Even the Prophet     was hurt by the slander/Kufr of Quraysh, Allah   Says:

      

Imam Ibn Jarir At-Tabari   comments on this Ayah and said:

The Exalted says  in His mention  to His Prophet Muhammad : And indeed We know, O Muhammad, that your chest is constricted by what these polytheists of your people say  by their denial of you, their mockery of you, and their mockery of what you have brought to them.

Its also mentioned by Ibn Abee ad-Dunya in his Al-Ishraf:

tim ibn Yay narrated to us, saying: I heard Muammad ibn Yay al-Marwaz say: A man said to Wahb ibn Munabbih, So-and-so has insulted you. He replied: Could the devil find no messenger other than you?

And these are widely cited although I couldnt find a Sanad for them, although due to its meaning, it is worthy of mentioning as a reminder for us all, InshaAllah:

Its said that a man came to a wise man and said: So-and-so has insulted you. He replied: He shot an arrow at me and it did not reach me  so why did you carry the arrow and lodge it in my heart?

And its said that a man came to al-Shfi  may Allah have mercy on him  and said: So-and-so speaks ill of you. He replied: If you are telling the truth, then you are a tale-carrier (Nammm); and if you are lying, then you are a transgressor. So the man was ashamed and left.

Defend your brothers in Islam, without hesitation, but be weary not fall into the trap of Nameemah unintentionally, lest you hurt your brother(s) unintentionally. If anything, if someone gets slandered often, be the bearer of good and positive news instead. Work to spread their Dawah, and make it widespread. Share with them the messages of support that flood social media during the slander, instead of the slander itself. 

Theres a wonderful website  thats been assembled, Allahumma Brek. Every time someone slanders the Shaykh, share this website. Let the hypocrites die in their rage."

"NEW: The confession of the perpetrator of the Tadamon Massacre, Amjad Yousesf:

 The individual appearing in the viral footage known as the Tadamon massacre is me, Amjad Youssef. These events date back to the early years of the Syrian revolution. 

 At the time, I was accompanied by Najib al-Halabi and a photographer whose name I cannot recall. We rounded up 40 individuals at random, under the pretext that they were 'terrorists'.

 We took them to a pre-dug pit, where I began executing them, as shown in the video. In some instances, I waited for the victim to fall into the pit before firing; in others, I shot them before they fell. 

 Intermittently, I would call on Najib al-Halabi to take his turn in the killing, until we had executed everyone present.

 We had previously placed car tires at the bottom of the pit. After the executions, we piled more tires over the bodies and set them on fire to prevent the scent of decomposition from spreading throughout the area. We then filled the pit. 

 I received no military orders to carry out this massacre, it was my own personal decision. 

 I cannot explain why there were exactly 40 victims, the selection was arbitrary, dictated solely by the circumstances at the time.

Source: Usman A Khan and translation by Syrian journalist Qusay Noor"

text:
"#ISIS #AlQaeda #Iran
After focusing on Syria in its 21 Feb leadership message, urging rival Islamists to join IS due to the Sharaa government's alleged subservience to US, IS has shifted its attention to Al-Qaeda. 

In the latest issue of its weekly paper Al-Naba on 26 Feb, IS claims Al-Qaeda is subservient to Iran, encouraging Al-Qaeda followers/sympathisers to wake up, and presumably switch to IS instead.

IS points to Al-Qaedas 4 Feb message, which called for attacks against US military bases in the Middle East, as evidence of this alleged loyalty to Iran. 
Many anti-Al-Qaeda  jihadist as well as analysts at the time interpreted this call as a sign of Al-Qaedas indirect alignment with Iran, particularly since Al-Qaeda leadership is believed to be based there.

By trying to discredit both Al-Qaeda and the Sharaa government in Syria - the rival global jihadist project and the pragmatic Islamist project, respectively - IS aims to position itself as the only true Muslim group that remains committed to Sharia and that has not allegedly compromised for political gains. 

IS wants to paint itself as the righteous choice for those seeking a genuine Muslim cause"

text:
"Abu Bakr, without any iota of doubt.

It is a matter upon which the scholars have long agreed. Whenever a virtue is found that distinguishes one of the Companions, you will find that Abu Bakr possessed an even greater share of that very quality.

When patience and generosity are mentioned, the mind often turns to Uthman. Abu Bakr combined both virtues in a measure even more remarkable.

When the knowledge of Hadith is discussed, one immediately thinks of Abu Hurayrah. Abu Bakr knows unimaginably more Hadith than Abu Hurayrah

And when courage, firmness, and resolve in the face of criticism are praised, names such as Umar, Ali, Khalid, and others naturally arise. Wallai, even among these towering figures, Abu Bakr remains ahead of them all.

He is the finest of mankind after the Prophets.
May Allah be pleased with him and with all the noble Companions. Ameen"

text:
"Salafis do not believe that the pagans of Makkah possessed a complete understanding of Tawheed al-Rububiyyah. When they assert that the pagans affirmed Tawheed al-Rububiyyah, they are referring to its foundational aspect (asl), which is the belief in one God as the ultimate creator and sustainer of the universe, the only being who is entirely independent. Salafis emphasize this point in response to saint venerators who argue that they are not guilty of shirk simply because they do not consider their saints to be independent of Allah. Salafis contend that the independence of a being is not the sole criterion for determining whether certain beliefs or actions constitute major shirk.

Salafis are well aware that the pagan Quraysh, while acknowledging Allah as the supreme creator, also attributed daughters to Him, worshipped idols, and failed to affirm His attributes correctly. These theological errors are considered distortions of Tawheed al-Rububiyyah.

Prominent Salafi scholars, such as Shaykh Abdulrahman Al-Barrak and Shaykh Yusuf Al-Ghafees, have argued that saint venerators also commit shirk in Rububiyyah by ascribing certain powers to their saints, powers that should be attributed exclusively to Allah.

To avoid semantic confusion, if Rububiyyah is primarily understood as Allahs independence and self-sufficiency, then Salafis assert that most of the pagan Quraysh acknowledged this foundational aspect. (However, Salafi scholars such as Shaykh Sultan al-Umayri, Abdulrazzaq Abu Badr, and Abu Bakr Muhammad Zakariyyah note that the Quraysh were not a homogeneous group and differed in their beliefs.) If Rububiyyah is defined in broader terms, while still rooted in the foundation of Allahs independence, Salafis maintain that the pagans only affirmed this foundational aspect.

The central issue, according to Salafis, is the affirmation of Allahs independence and the rejection of any justification by saint venerators that their actions do not constitute major shirk."
